content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 17,
1993
Honorable Willie L.
Brown
Speaker of the Assembly
State Capitol,
Room 219
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Honorable David Roberti
Senate President Pro Tern
State Capitol,
Room 205
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Honorable James L.
Brulte
Assembly Minority Leader
State Capitol,
Room 2114
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Honorable Ken Maddy
Senate Minority Leader
State Capitol,
Room 305
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-040
Dear Sirs:
You have requested advice concerning the application of gift
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(“the Act”)
1 to Members of
the California State Legislature and legislative staff regarding
attendance at the 1993 National Conference of State Legislatures.
QUESTION
Do the various benefits provided to public officials who pay
full registration fees to the 1993 Annual Meeting of the National
Conference of State Legislatures
(NCSL),
including seminars,
meals, social events,
and shuttle transportation, constitute
“gifts” to these public officials?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-040
Page
2
CONCLUSION
The various benefits provided at the Annual Meeting of the
National Conference of State Legislatures may constitute gifts to
the public officials to the extent that the costs of the tangible
benefits received exceed the aggregate sum of the registration fee
plus any dues paid as a requirement of participation.
FACTS
The State of California is the host state this year for the
Annual Meeting of the NCSL.
The Annual Meeting is scheduled to be
held in San Diego, California from July 25 through July 30,
1993.
The NCSL is a nonpartisan national organization concerned
with public policy.
Its purpose is to “promote the improvement
and effectiveness of state legislatures and represent states and
their legislatures in the federal system of government.”
Donations to NCSL have been held to qualify as tax exempt
“charitable contributions” under Internal Revenue Code Section
170, pursuant to a series of Internal Revenue Service
(IRS)
“letter rulings.”
In addition,
NCSL has an adjunct
(501) (c) (3)
foundation.
The Annual Meetings of the NCSL are attended by legislators,
legislative staff,
and other public officials and employees
representing a wide variety of political and policy points of
view,
from every state in the union.
Distinguished speakers are
invited and seminars are held on a variety of public policy
topics.
As the “host state” for the 1993 Annual Meeting,
California
must establish a “host state entity.”
That entity must accept
certain responsibilities relative to hosting the event.
Those
responsibilities include the financing of all local transportation
(in the form of shuttle bus service among various event sites)
for
attendees, renting of the convention hall, hosting of two
receptions for attendees,
and related expenses.
It is expected that the “host committee” will be formed in
the following manner:
1)
A nonprofit corporation which will act as the “host
committee” will be established.
The committee’s articles of
incorporation will provide that its purpose is to receive and
expend private and public funds solely for the purpose of hosting
the Annual Meeting.
2)
The “host committee” will seek and obtain an IRS “letter
ruling” which will state that contributions to the “host
committee” are tax deductible as charitable contributions.
It is
expected that the IRS will treat the NCSL and the “host committee”
as one and the same for purposes of tax exemption for
contributions.
Private and public donations will then be sought


Our File No. A-93-040
Page
3
by the “host committee” to help underwrite portions pf the Annual
Meeting.
3)
The “host committee” will account to NCSL for all
contributions and expenditures received and expended on behalf of
the Annual Meeting.
Any excess contribution funds will be
refunded to the host state,
up to the amount expended by the host
state (if any)
frompublic funds in connection with the meeting.
Any remaining funds will be contributed to the NCSL’s nonprofit
adjunct foundation.
4)
The host committee will go out of existence as soon as the
financial affairs of the Annual Meeting in San Diego are
concluded.
In addition to donations solicited by the “host committee,”
the Annual Meeting will also be funded by registration fees and
NCSL dues paid by each state.
Registrants who are from the various state legislatures
(public sector registrants) will pay $300 in registration fees.
Any member of the public
(private sector registrant)
who is not a
dues-paying NCSL member may attend the Annual Meeting,
but will be
charged a registration fee of $600.
Assurances have been received
from NCSL representatives involved in planning the Annual Meeting
that the public sector
(member) registrants will pay
(through
registration fees and dues)
the equivalent of that paid as the
private sector registration fee.
This equivalent amount will be
paid in two “components” by their respective agencies.
The first
component will be paid from the dues given to NCSL by each state,
and the second component will come from the registration fees.
The fees will provide for admission to all Annual Meeting
events,
including the meals,
social events,
and seminars.
Lodging
and out—of—town travel will not be included and must be provided
by the registrant or his or her agency.
However, shuttle bus
service to the various event locations will be included in the
registration fee.
All registrants will be entitled to participate in all events
and activities and will receive the same benefits (i.e., there
will be no differentiation between the benefits for registrants
who pay $300 and those who pay a $600 registration fee).
ANALYSIS
As amended effective January 1,
1991, the Act limits gifts
received by elected state officers and designated employees of the
California Legislature to $250 in each calendar year from each
single source.
2
(Sections 89504 and 89505.)
2
The gift limitation amount will be adjusted biennially.
(Section 89504.)


Our File No.
A-93-040
Page
4
Section 82028(a)
defines a “gift” as:
[A]ny payment to the extent that consideration
of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of
the public without regard to official status.
Any
person,
other than a defendant in a criminal action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by reason of
receipt of consideration has the burden of proving
that the consideration received is of equal or
greater value.
You stated in your letter that assurances have been received
from NCSIJ representatives involved in planning the Annual Meeting
that the public sector
(member) registrants will pay the
equivalent of the registration fee paid by the private sector
registrants.
The public sector registrants, however, will pay
through two components by their respective agencies; the first
component through dues and the second component through a $300
registration fee.
Thus,
we assume for the purposes of this
analysis that the first dues component paid on behalf of the
California public sector participants will amount to a sum of
$300.
To the extent that the amount of this first dues component
is less than $300, the difference between the $300 and the actual
amount paid constitutes a gift.
You have stated in your letter that additional public and
private donations will be sought to help underwrite portions of
the Annual Meeting.
We assume from this information that the
actual cost of the Annual Meeting will exceed $600 per
participant.
Thus, the donations which cover event costs in
excess of $600 may constitute a gift to the public officials as
well, if these donations confer tangible direct benefits to
participants beyond mere “incidentals.”
A caveat:
Care should be taken to avoid classifying a
nonpolitical nonprofit organization such as the one you have
described as a “controlled committee” if the donations are
received at the behest of candidates for elective office.
Regulation 18217
(copy enclosed)
provides you with the necessary
guidance to ensure that the nonprofit organization does not become
a controlled committee.


Our File No.
A-93-040
Page
5
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosure
Original copy sent to Honorable Willie L.
Brown,
Honorable David
Roberti,
Honorable James L.
Brulte,
and Honorable Ken Maddy.
